schools.summary: Henry: 'he that believeth doth not make haste'; election does not slacken but quickens pious endeavour.
K&D make it the chapter header; Ellicott unfolds Hebron — Abraham's home, the patriarchs' burial, a priestly city of refuge.
Wesley: David asked not whether but where to take the kingdom, enquiring by Urim; Clarke notes Hebron's vineyards and springs.
Gill: David enquired through Abiathar and the breastplate before making any move.

debates.question: By what instrument did David inquire of the Lord?

sides.position: by the breastplate of judgment brought by Abiathar the priest

schools.summary: Gill measures the sixteen miles from Ziklag to Hebron: obedience follows inquiry without delay.
K&D set the founding of the kingdom in God's 'grace and truth'; Ellicott anchors the two wives in 1 Samuel 25.
Gill: David surely provided well for the faithful men who had risked their all with him.
Ellicott: David's whole force of 600 men with families made permanent settlement in the towns of Hebron.
Wesley: they dwelt in the towns belonging to Hebron, the metropolis being filled with priests and David's court.
Clarke: Samuel's anointing gave jus ad regnum, Judah's gives jus in regno; Wesley marks Judah's modesty in anointing him over one tribe only.
Ellicott counts three anointings and recalls the old bond of Jabesh-gilead with Benjamin and Saul (Judges 21).
Gill: the principal men, the elders of each city, came to anoint him.
Gill: David thanks Jabesh for rescuing and burying the bodies of Saul and his sons.
Clarke: a noble act showing the grandeur of David's mind, honouring Saul as the Lord's anointed despite the wrong; Wesley: to bury the dead is an act of mercy.
Gill hears a prayer for real, substantial blessing — 'blessings indeed.'
K&D tie 'grace and truth' to Exodus 34:6; Ellicott reads the Hebrew as a present act ('I show you this good') and frank wise policy.
Wesley: true kindness in deeds not words; 'I will requite' shows David far from offended at their loyalty to his late enemy.
Gill: David steadies a people fearful of Philistine reprisal — be of good courage.
Clarke: David moves to attach brave Jabesh before it clings to Saul's house (their old debt over Nahash); Wesley: 'I will defend you,' though your shepherd is dead.
K&D join David's appeal to the ominous notice that Abner was at that moment setting up Ishbosheth.
Henry: the man God made king vs the man Abner made king; a type of Messiah's kingdom against which rulers take counsel (Ps 2).
Gill: Abner, Saul's cousin and general, acted to keep his own command.
Wesley: Abner's ambition — Ishbosheth would have the name, Abner the power; Clarke: Abner envious of David.
Ellicott and K&D on Abner's kinship and office, and the name Eshbaal changed to Ishbosheth (shame); Mahanaim of Jacob's story.
Ellicott and K&D read the list of territories as gradual conquest as the land was freed from the Philistines, ending 'but Judah followed David.'
Gill: Gilead beyond Jordan — Gad, Reuben, half Manasseh — first acknowledged Ishbosheth.
Wesley and Clarke take the 'Ashurites' for the tribe of Asher (per the Chaldee); Abner kept the king from places of resistance until his army recruited.

sides.position: the tribe of Asher (per the Chaldee paraphrast)
uncertain — probably men of a tribe Ashur

schools.summary: Gill: Ishbosheth was forty, born the year his father began to reign.
Clarke canvasses the theories reconciling the 'two years' with David's seven and a half (incl. Houbigant's emendation to six); Wesley: two years before the war broke out.
K&D insist the text plainly gives David seven and a half years at Hebron, against schemes that would divide it.

debates.question: How can Ishbosheth's 'two years' fit David's seven and a half at Hebron?

sides.position: two years mark the peaceful interval before the war, not the whole reign
emend 'two years' to 'six years' (Houbigant), though unsupported by the versions

themes: two years vs seven and a half

schools.summary: Gill stresses David reigned in Hebron over the house of Judah 'and over them only' — the kingdom still divided.
K&D take the seven and a half years at face value and let the verse run into Abner's march on Gibeon to subdue Judah.
Gill: Abner, once Saul's captain and now Ishbosheth's, leads out the aggression; David's men only respond.
Ellicott gives Gibeon's history (the league by craft, Joshua 9) and weighs whether the meeting was by design or a race for Benjamin; K&D read Abner's aim to make Judah subject.
Gill names Joab, son of David's sister Zeruiah, general of David's army.
Ellicott on Joab's later rise (1 Chron 11:6) and the pool's dimensions; K&D picture the two armies seated on opposite banks and read 'play' as single combat.
Wesley: the hosts met and put themselves in a posture for battle.
Clarke calls it 'diabolical play'; Wesley: Abner speaks like a vain-glorious, cruel soldier of fortune — 'unworthy the name of a man.'
Henry: 'Fools thus make a mock at sin'; Joab lacked resolution to refuse the challenge.
Ellicott reads Abner more charitably — a champions' combat to spare tribal bloodshed (cp. the Horatii and Curiatii); K&D: probably to avoid civil war.
Gill: Joab held back, his orders from David being only defensive.

debates.question: What was Abner's intent in proposing the 'play'?

sides.position: wanton cruelty — a jest made of bloodshed
a calculated attempt to limit slaughter by a combat of champions

schools.summary: Gill: Abner hand-picked twelve stout Benjamites, his own kinsmen, matched by twelve of David's servants.
K&D read the mutual, instantaneous slaughter — each sword already in the other's side — and the symmetry that ensures the duel decides nothing.
Gill: each caught his fellow by the hair of the head before thrusting home.
Clarke (the beard, as Alexander's men shaved theirs) and Wesley ('the field of rock') debate the name and admire the rock-like firmness of the slain.
Ellicott judges the etymology doubtful — 'field of strong men' or 'field of sharp edges.'
Henry: 'poor honour' bought at such cost, against the promise that they who lose their lives for Christ shall find them.

sides.position: the field of strong / rocky men (their firmness)
the portion of the mighty, or the inheritance of the slain (per the Targum)
the field of sharp edges (of the swords)

schools.summary: Gill: the deadlock of the champions gives way to a furious general battle in which Abner is beaten.
Ellicott: small in casualties (19 vs 360) but 'a turning-point'; K&D single out Asahel's death for its consequences.
Henry: David had God on his side, so his side was victorious, and the challenger went away with loss.
Henry: Asahel's rash pride — 'let not the swift glory in swiftness'; death comes by ways we least suspect, and our prized gifts betray us.
K&D: the three sons of Zeruiah; Asahel light of foot as a wild roe, pursuing Abner without turning aside.
Clarke: swiftness of foot was a prized heroic accomplishment — Homer's 'swift-footed' Achilles.
Gill notes the three brothers were in the battle.
Gill: ambitious of glory, Asahel trusts his speed, forgetting the race is not to the swift and that he faces a veteran.
Ellicott: the youngest nephew sought Abner out, overtook him from behind; the spear's sharpened butt (1 Sam 26:7); 'fifth rib' = abdomen.
Gill: Abner, sensing one hard at his heels, turns to identify his pursuer — the pause of a veteran taking his measure.
Gill: Abner pleads out of respect for Asahel and dread of Joab.
K&D: 'seize one of the young men and take his armour' — an honourable prize offered so Abner need not kill him.
Clarke: Asahel coveted Abner's armour as a trophy; Wesley: 'go to some young and raw soldier,' desist from an old captain.
Henry: it is wisdom to weigh our strength against an adversary's (Luke 14:31); commendable that enemies show mutual respect.
Gill: Abner spoke 'again,' loath to dispatch him.
K&D preserve Abner's twofold plea — spare thy life, and how could I face Joab thy brother? — a dread that proves prophetic.
Clarke: this slaying will cost Abner his own life in the next chapter.
Gill: determined to take Abner captive, Asahel refused to turn, and fell to the spear that came out behind him.
K&D: only after the warning did Abner wound him with the pointed butt of the spear in the abdomen.
Henry: death by a back-stroke he never guarded against — betrayed by the swiftness he presumed on, he ran upon his death.
Wesley echoes: his swiftness only forwarded his fate.
Gill: 'but Joab and Abishai' — not standing still like the rest — pursued Abner to avenge their brother.
K&D and Ellicott locate the chase at the unknown hill Ammah before Giah, by the wilderness of Gibeon, and judge the pursuit not long; the sun sets.
Gill: the scattered Benjamites regroup into one troop on a hilltop to beat back the pursuers.
Ellicott: Abner rallies his own and Saul's kinsmen, the Benjamites, in a strong position.
Henry: the man forward to fight is first to beg peace — reason is easy when it makes for us; the issue of things alters men's minds.

themes: the maker of the quarrel sues for peace

schools.summary: Gill: with a troop and a hilltop, Abner can safely halt and call across to Joab.
K&D: Abner warns of a desperate struggle's bitter end; Ellicott suspects he already weighs transferring allegiance to David, his sense quickened by defeat.
Wesley: they are 'brethren, by nation and religion,' not to be pursued like Philistines.
Clarke and Wesley: had Abner not provoked the fight (v14), Joab (on the defensive) would not have attacked — Abner is the sole cause.
K&D agree Joab throws all blame on Abner; Ellicott records a second reading — Joab would have pursued only till morning, but stops since Abner sues.
Gill: 'As God liveth' is the form of an oath by the living God.

debates.question: What does Joab mean by 'unless thou hadst spoken'?

sides.position: Abner alone caused the battle by his challenge; the blame is his
Joab meant to pursue only till morning, but is content to stop now that Abner sues

schools.summary: Gill: the trumpet blown as a signal of retreat; the people stood still.
Henry: though conqueror, Joab generously grants the halt, knowing David's aversion to bloodshed — 'good husbandry to be sparing of blood.'
K&D trace Abner's night retreat through the Arabah and Bithron to Mahanaim; Ellicott: 'no more' means only this campaign, for a long war followed (2 Sam 3:1).

themes: a battle ended, not the war

schools.summary: Gill: Abner marched all night lest Joab return to avenge his brother.
Ellicott traces the route up the Jordan valley, across a ford, through Bithron to Mahanaim, whence he had set out.
Clarke: this was the commencement of the civil wars, the division of the two kingdoms that deluged both nations with blood.
Gill: Joab turned back under commission from David to shed as little blood as he could.
K&D and Ellicott explain the disparity (19 and Asahel vs 360) — David's tried men against the depleted remnant of Saul's beaten army, plus the pursuer's advantage.
Henry: Asahel is named apart because he 'was worth more than all.'

themes: tried men vs a beaten remnant

schools.summary: Gill: 360 of Benjamin and Abner's men fell, as their bodies on the field showed.
K&D fold the count into the homeward march, David's men bearing Asahel to burial while leaving the enemy dead on the field.
Gill (with Josephus): the brothers took up Asahel after burying the common dead.
Ellicott reconstructs the timing and the thirteen-mile night march; K&D: 'it got light to them at Hebron' — they reached it at daybreak.
Henry: distinctions made between the dust of some and others, but in the resurrection only the godly/ungodly difference remains for ever.
